1.text: In the beginning of the fortieth year of the reign of the judges over the Nephite people, a serious conflict arose among them.
Pahoran had died, and a serious dispute broke out over who should become the next chief judge. The ones fighting over it were his own sons.
These are the names of those who competed for the judgment seat, causing the people to take sides: Pahoran, Paanchi, and Pacumeni.
These were not all of Pahoran's sons (he had many), but these three were the ones who competed for the judgment seat, and they caused three divisions among the people.
Nevertheless, Pahoran was chosen by the vote of the people to be the chief judge and governor over the Nephite people.
When Pacumeni saw that he could not win the judgment seat, he accepted the people's decision and supported the outcome.
But Paanchi and his supporters were extremely angry. He was about to persuade them to rebel against the rest of the people.
As he was about to do this, he was arrested, tried by the voice of the people, and sentenced to death for rising up in rebellion and trying to destroy the people's liberty.
When Paanchi's supporters saw that he had been sentenced to death, they were furious. They sent a man named Kishkumen to the judgment seat, and he murdered Pahoran while he sat there.
Pahoran's servants chased Kishkumen, but he escaped so quickly that no one could catch him.
Kishkumen went back to those who had sent him, and they all made a covenant, swearing by their everlasting Creator that none of them would reveal that Kishkumen had murdered Pahoran.

Because of this, Kishkumen was not known among the people of Nephi, since he had been disguised when he murdered Pahoran. Kishkumen and his followers who had made a covenant with him mixed in among the people in a way that they could not all be found. But as many of them as were found were condemned to death.

Pacumeni was then chosen by the voice of the people to be chief judge and governor in place of his brother Pahoran, which was his right. All of this happened in the fortieth year of the judges, and so it ended.
In the forty-first year of the judges, the Lamanites gathered together a huge army armed with swords, curved blades, bows, arrows, helmets, breastplates, and shields of every kind.
They came down to fight against the Nephites. Their leader was a man named Coriantumr, a descendant of Zarahemla who had left the Nephites. He was a large and powerful man.
The Lamanite king, Tubaloth (the son of Ammoron), believed that Coriantumr was mighty enough, with his strength and great wisdom, to defeat the Nephites and gain power over them.
So Tubaloth stirred the Lamanites up to anger, gathered his armies, appointed Coriantumr as their leader, and sent them marching to the land of Zarahemla to battle the Nephites.

Because there had been so much conflict and difficulty in the government, the Nephites had not posted enough guards in Zarahemla. They had assumed the Lamanites would never dare attack such a great city in the heart of their lands.
But Coriantumr marched forward at the head of his enormous army and attacked the people of the city with such incredible speed that the Nephites had no time to gather their armies.
Coriantumr cut down the guards at the entrance of the city and marched in with his whole army. They killed everyone who resisted and took control of the entire city.
Pacumeni, the chief judge, tried to flee from Coriantumr but was cornered at the city walls. Coriantumr struck him against the wall, and he died. And so ended the life of Pacumeni.

When Coriantumr realized he controlled the city of Zarahemla and saw that the Nephites had fled, been killed, or been thrown into prison, and that he held the strongest fortress in all the land, he became bold enough to try to conquer the entire land.
He did not stay in Zarahemla but marched with a large army toward the city of Bountiful. He was determined to fight his way through and take control of the northern parts of the land.
He assumed the Nephites' greatest strength was in the center of the land, so he marched through quickly, giving them no time to gather except in small groups, and he cut them down as he went.
But Coriantumr's march through the center of the land actually gave Moronihah a great advantage over him, despite the many Nephites who had been killed.
Moronihah had expected the Lamanites to attack the border cities as they had done before, so he had positioned his strong armies along the borders.

But the Lamanites were not afraid as Moronihah had expected. They had invaded the center of the land, captured the capital city of Zarahemla, and were marching through the most important areas, killing men, women, and children with great slaughter, and taking many cities and strongholds.
When Moronihah learned what was happening, he immediately sent Lehi with an army to cut off the Lamanites before they could reach the land of Bountiful.
Lehi succeeded in blocking them before they reached Bountiful and fought them, forcing them to retreat back toward Zarahemla.
Moronihah then blocked their retreat and fought them in an extremely bloody battle. Many were killed, and among the dead was Coriantumr himself.
The Lamanites could not escape in any direction—north, south, east, or west—because the Nephites had surrounded them on every side.
Coriantumr had led the Lamanites right into the middle of the Nephite forces. He was killed, and the Lamanites surrendered to the Nephites.
Moronihah took back the city of Zarahemla and allowed the captured Lamanites to leave the land in peace.
And so ended the forty-first year of the reign of the judges.

2.text: In the forty-second year of the judges, after Moronihah had restored peace between the Nephites and Lamanites, there was no one to fill the judgment seat, and so another dispute began over who should become the chief judge.
Helaman, the son of Helaman, was chosen by the vote of the people to fill the judgment seat.
But Kishkumen, who had murdered the previous Pahoran, was plotting to kill Helaman too. He was supported by his band, who had all made a covenant to keep his crimes secret.
There was a man named Gadianton who was extremely skilled with words and very crafty in carrying out secret murder and robbery. He became the leader of Kishkumen's band.
Gadianton promised them that if they placed him in the judgment seat, he would give the members of his band positions of power and authority among the people. So Kishkumen set out to kill Helaman.
As Kishkumen was heading to the judgment seat to kill Helaman, one of Helaman's servants had been out at night and, through a disguise, had learned about the band's plan to destroy Helaman.
This servant met Kishkumen and gave him a secret sign. Kishkumen then told him his plan and asked the servant to lead him to the judgment seat so he could murder Helaman.

When Helaman's servant learned the full plan of Kishkumen—that his goal and the goal of his entire band was to murder, rob, and gain power (this was their secret plan and organization)—the servant said to Kishkumen: Let us go to the judgment seat.

This pleased Kishkumen greatly, because he thought his plan was working. But as they walked toward the judgment seat, Helaman's servant stabbed Kishkumen in the heart, and he fell dead without a sound. The servant then ran and told Helaman everything he had seen, heard, and done.
Helaman sent out men to capture the band of robbers and secret murderers so they could be punished according to the law.

But when Gadianton realized that Kishkumen had not returned, he was afraid he too would be caught. So he ordered his band to follow him, and they fled into the wilderness by a secret route. When Helaman's men went to find them, they were nowhere to be found.
More will be said about Gadianton later. And so ended the forty-second year of the reign of the judges over the Nephite people.
By the end of this book, you will see that Gadianton brought about the overthrow and nearly the complete destruction of the Nephite people.
I do not mean the end of the book of Helaman, but the end of the book of Nephi, from which I have taken everything I have written.

3.text: In the forty-third year of the judges, there was no conflict among the Nephites except for a little pride in the church, which caused some minor disagreements. These issues were settled by the end of the year.
There was no conflict among the people in the forty-fourth year, and very little in the forty-fifth year either.
In the forty-sixth year, there was much conflict and many disagreements. A very large number of people left the land of Zarahemla and traveled to the land northward to settle there.
They traveled an extremely great distance, arriving at large bodies of water and many rivers.
They spread out into every part of the land that had not already been left empty and without trees because of the many people who had lived there before.
No part of the land was truly empty, except for its lack of timber. It was called desolate because of the great destruction of the people who had lived there before.
Since there was very little timber on the land, the people who settled there became extremely skilled at making cement. They built cement houses to live in.
They multiplied and spread from the land southward to the land northward until they covered the whole face of the earth, from the south sea to the north sea, and from the west sea to the east sea.

The people in the land northward lived in tents and cement houses. They let every tree that sprouted grow, so that over time they would have timber to build their houses, cities, temples, synagogues, sanctuaries, and other buildings.
Because timber was so scarce in the land northward, they shipped large amounts of it there by sea.
This allowed the people in the land northward to build many cities, both of wood and of cement.
Many of the people of Ammon, who were Lamanites by birth, also went into this land.
Many records were kept about the activities of these people—detailed and very extensive records.

But even a hundredth part of the history of this people—the story of the Lamanites and Nephites, their wars, conflicts, disagreements, preaching, prophecies, shipping, shipbuilding, temple construction, their righteousness and wickedness, murders, robberies, plundering, and all kinds of sins—cannot be included in this work.
There are many books and records of every kind, and they have been kept mostly by the Nephites.

These records have been passed down from one generation to the next by the Nephites, until they fell into sin and were murdered, plundered, hunted, driven out, killed, and scattered across the land. They mixed with the Lamanites until they were no longer called Nephites, becoming wicked, wild, and fierce—becoming like the Lamanites.
Now I return to my account. Everything I have just described happened after there had been great conflict, disturbances, wars, and disagreements among the Nephite people.
The forty-sixth year of the reign of the judges came to an end.
There was still great conflict in the land during the forty-seventh year and also in the forty-eighth year.

Nevertheless, Helaman led with justice and fairness from the judgment seat. He kept the laws, judgments, and commandments of God. He always did what was right in the sight of God and followed the ways of his father, and because of this he prospered in the land.
He had two sons. He named the older one Nephi and the younger one Lehi. They began to grow up in the ways of the Lord.
The wars and conflicts began to decrease slightly among the Nephites toward the end of the forty-eighth year of the judges.

In the forty-ninth year, lasting peace was established in the land—except for the secret organizations that Gadianton the robber had set up in the more settled areas, which were unknown to those in charge of the government and therefore were not destroyed.
In that same year, the church experienced tremendous growth, with thousands joining and being baptized in repentance.
The prosperity of the church was so great, and so many blessings were poured out on the people, that even the high priests and teachers were amazed beyond measure.
The work of the Lord prospered as many souls were baptized and joined the church of God—even tens of thousands.
From this we can see that the Lord is merciful to everyone who sincerely calls upon His holy name.
We can see that the gate of heaven is open to all—even to everyone who will believe in the name of Jesus Christ, who is the Son of God.

We see that whoever chooses to may take hold of the word of God, which is living and powerful, and which can cut through all the tricks, traps, and schemes of the devil, guiding the follower of Christ on a straight and narrow path across the great gulf of misery prepared to swallow up the wicked.
The word of God will bring their souls—their immortal souls—to the right hand of God in the kingdom of heaven, to sit with Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the holy prophets, never to leave again.
In this year there was continual rejoicing in the land of Zarahemla and in all the surrounding regions—throughout all the land the Nephites possessed.
There was peace and very great joy for the rest of the forty-ninth year, and also continual peace and great joy in the fiftieth year of the judges.

In the fifty-first year there was peace as well, except for the pride that began to enter the church—not into the church of God itself, but into the hearts of the people who claimed to belong to the church.
They became proud, even to the point of persecuting many of their fellow members. This was a great evil that caused the more humble people to suffer terrible persecution and much hardship.

Nevertheless, the humble believers fasted and prayed often and grew stronger and stronger in humility and firmer and firmer in their faith in Christ. Their souls were filled with joy and comfort, and their hearts were purified and made holy because they surrendered their hearts to God.
The fifty-second year also ended in peace, except for the enormous pride that had entered the people's hearts because of their great wealth and prosperity. This pride grew worse day by day.

In the fifty-third year of the judges, Helaman died, and his oldest son Nephi began to serve in his place. He led from the judgment seat with justice and fairness, keeping God's commandments and following in his father's footsteps.

4.text: In the fifty-fourth year, there were many divisions in the church and conflict among the people, leading to much bloodshed.
The rebellious ones were killed or driven out of the land, and they went to the Lamanite king.
They tried to stir up the Lamanites to war against the Nephites, but the Lamanites were too afraid and would not listen to the dissenters.

In the fifty-sixth year, more Nephite dissenters went to the Lamanites, and together with the earlier group they succeeded in stirring the Lamanites to anger against the Nephites. They spent the whole year preparing for war.
In the fifty-seventh year they attacked the Nephites, and by the fifty-eighth year they had taken the land of Zarahemla and all the lands up to the border of the land of Bountiful.
The Nephites and Moronihah's armies were driven all the way back into the land of Bountiful.
There they built fortifications against the Lamanites, stretching from the west sea to the east sea—a line about a day's journey for a Nephite—to defend their territory in the north.
The Nephite dissenters, with the help of a large Lamanite army, had taken all the Nephite lands in the south. All of this happened in the fifty-eighth and fifty-ninth years of the judges.
In the sixtieth year, Moronihah's armies succeeded in recapturing many parts of the land and many cities that had fallen to the Lamanites.
In the sixty-first year, they managed to regain half of all their former territory.

This terrible loss and great slaughter among the Nephites would not have happened if not for their wickedness and the sins that existed among them—even among those who claimed to belong to the church of God.

It was because of the pride in their hearts, their extreme wealth, their mistreatment of the poor, withholding food from the hungry and clothing from the needy, mocking sacred things, rejecting the spirit of prophecy and revelation, committing murder, robbery, lying, stealing, adultery, stirring up conflicts, and leaving to join the Lamanites.

Because of their great wickedness and their boasting in their own strength, they were left to rely on their own power. As a result, they did not prosper but were afflicted, defeated, and driven back by the Lamanites until they had lost almost all their lands.
Moronihah preached to the people about their sins, and Nephi and Lehi, the sons of Helaman, also preached and prophesied many things about what would happen if they did not repent.
And they did repent, and as they repented, they began to prosper again.
When Moronihah saw that they were repenting, he led them from place to place and from city to city until they had regained half of their lands and half of their property.
And so ended the sixty-first year of the reign of the judges.
In the sixty-second year, Moronihah could not take back any more territory from the Lamanites.
They gave up trying to recover the rest of their lands because the Lamanites were too numerous. Moronihah used all his forces to hold onto the lands they had regained.
Because the Lamanites were so numerous, the Nephites lived in great fear that they would be overpowered, crushed, and destroyed.
They began to remember the prophecies of Alma and the words of Mosiah. They realized that they had been a stubborn people who had ignored the commandments of God.

They had changed and trampled the laws of Mosiah—the laws the Lord had commanded him to give the people. They saw that their laws had become corrupt and that they had become a wicked people, as wicked as the Lamanites.
Because of their sins, the church had begun to shrink. They had stopped believing in the spirit of prophecy and revelation, and the judgments of God stared them in the face.
They realized they had become weak, just like the Lamanites, and that the Spirit of the Lord no longer protected them. It had left them because the Spirit of the Lord does not dwell in unholy places.

The Lord stopped protecting them with His miraculous power because they had fallen into unbelief and terrible wickedness. They saw that the Lamanites far outnumbered them, and unless they held firmly to the Lord their God, they would certainly be destroyed.
They could see that the Lamanites' strength was equal to their own, man for man. They had fallen into this terrible condition because of their sins, and it had happened in just a few years.

5.text: In that same year, Nephi gave up the judgment seat to a man named Cezoram.

Since the laws and government were established by the voice of the people, and those who chose evil now outnumbered those who chose good, the nation was heading toward destruction because the laws had become corrupt.
They were such a stubborn people that they could not be governed by law or justice without it leading to their destruction.
Nephi had grown weary of their wickedness, so he gave up the judgment seat and devoted the rest of his life to preaching the word of God. His brother Lehi did the same for the rest of his days.
They remembered the words their father Helaman had spoken to them. These are the words he said:

My sons, I want you to remember to keep the commandments of God, and I want you to share these words with the people. I have given you the names of our first ancestors who came from Jerusalem. I did this so that when you remember your names, you will remember them; and when you remember them, you will remember what they did; and when you remember what they did, you will know that it has been said and written that they were good.
Therefore, my sons, I want you to do what is good, so that it may be said and written about you, just as it was said and written about them.

My sons, I have one more thing to ask of you: do not do good things just so you can boast about them, but do them to store up treasure in heaven—treasure that is eternal and never fades away—so that you may receive the precious gift of eternal life, which we have reason to believe was given to our ancestors.

O remember, remember, my sons, the words that King Benjamin spoke to his people. Remember that there is no other way for people to be saved except through the atoning blood of Jesus Christ, who will come. Remember that He comes to redeem the world.

Remember also what Amulek said to Zeezrom in the city of Ammonihah: he said that the Lord would surely come to redeem His people, but not to redeem them while they remained in their sins—rather, to redeem them from their sins.

The Father has given Christ power to redeem people from their sins through repentance. That is why He has sent angels to teach about the conditions of repentance, which lead people to the power of the Redeemer and the salvation of their souls.

And now, my sons, remember that you must build your foundation on the rock of our Redeemer, who is Christ, the Son of God. Then when the devil sends his powerful winds, his arrows in the whirlwind, and his hail and mighty storms against you, they will have no power to drag you down to the gulf of misery and endless suffering, because of the rock you are built upon. It is a sure foundation, and whoever builds on it cannot fall.
These were the words Helaman taught his sons. He also taught them many things that are not written, as well as many that are.
They remembered his words, and so they went out keeping the commandments of God, teaching the word of God among all the Nephite people, beginning at the city of Bountiful.
From there they went to the city of Gid, and from the city of Gid to the city of Mulek.
They went from one city to another until they had preached among all the Nephites in the land southward. Then they went into the land of Zarahemla, among the Lamanites.

They preached with great power, so much so that they convinced many of the Nephite dissenters who had joined the Lamanites. These dissenters came forward, confessed their sins, were baptized in repentance, and immediately returned to the Nephites to try to make right the wrongs they had done.
Nephi and Lehi preached to the Lamanites with such great power and authority—for they had been given both the power to speak and the words to say.

They spoke to the great astonishment of the Lamanites, convincing so many that eight thousand Lamanites in and around the land of Zarahemla were baptized in repentance and became convinced that the traditions of their fathers were wrong.
Nephi and Lehi then traveled to the land of Nephi.
There they were captured by a Lamanite army and thrown into prison—the very same prison where Ammon and his brothers had been held by the servants of Limhi.
After they had been in prison many days without food, their captors came to take them out and kill them.
But Nephi and Lehi were surrounded as if by fire, so that no one dared touch them for fear of being burned. Yet Nephi and Lehi were not burned. They stood in the middle of the fire and were unharmed.
When the people saw that Nephi and Lehi were encircled by a pillar of fire that did not burn them, they took courage.
The Lamanites did not dare to touch them or even come near them. They stood there struck silent with amazement.
Nephi and Lehi stepped forward and said: Do not be afraid. God has shown you this incredible thing to demonstrate that you cannot lay your hands on us to kill us.

When they had spoken these words, the earth shook violently and the prison walls trembled as if they were about to collapse—but they did not fall. Those in the prison included both Lamanites and Nephite dissenters.
A cloud of darkness covered them, and a deep, solemn fear came over them.
Then a voice came from above the cloud of darkness, saying: Repent, repent, and stop trying to destroy my servants whom I have sent to bring you good news.
When they heard this voice, they realized it was not the sound of thunder or a loud, crashing noise. It was a quiet voice of perfect gentleness, like a whisper, and yet it pierced to the very soul.
Despite how gentle the voice was, the earth shook violently and the prison walls trembled again as if about to collapse. The cloud of darkness did not go away.
The voice came again, saying: Repent, repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand. Stop trying to destroy my servants. Then the earth shook again and the walls trembled.
A third time the voice spoke, saying marvelous words that cannot be expressed by any person. The walls trembled again, and the earth shook as if it were about to split apart.
The Lamanites could not escape because the cloud of darkness surrounded them, and they could not move because of the fear that gripped them.
Among them was a man who was a Nephite by birth and had once belonged to the church of God but had turned away from it.

He turned around and saw through the cloud of darkness the faces of Nephi and Lehi. Their faces shone brilliantly, like the faces of angels. He saw that they were looking up to heaven and appeared to be speaking to someone they could see.
This man called out to the crowd, telling them to turn and look. They were given the power to turn, and they saw the faces of Nephi and Lehi.
They asked the man: What do all these things mean? Who are these men talking to?
The man's name was Aminadab. He said to them: They are speaking with the angels of God.
The Lamanites asked him: What must we do so that this cloud of darkness will be taken away from us?

Aminadab told them: You must repent and cry out to the voice until you have faith in Christ, who was taught to you by Alma, Amulek, and Zeezrom. When you do this, the cloud of darkness will be removed from you.
They all began to cry out to the voice of the one who had shaken the earth. They cried out until the cloud of darkness was gone.
When they looked around and saw that the cloud of darkness had been removed, they discovered that every one of them was encircled by a pillar of fire.

Nephi and Lehi were in the middle of them, also surrounded by fire. They were as if standing in the middle of a blazing fire, yet it did not harm them or touch the prison walls. They were filled with a joy that was beyond words and full of glory.
The Holy Spirit of God came down from heaven and entered their hearts. They were filled as if with fire, and they were able to speak marvelous words.
A voice came to them—a pleasant voice, like a whisper—saying:
Peace, peace be unto you, because of your faith in my Well Beloved, who has been since the foundation of the world.
When they heard this, they looked up to see where the voice came from. They saw the heavens open, and angels came down from heaven and ministered to them.
About three hundred people saw and heard these things. They were told to go out and not marvel or doubt.

They went out and shared with the people throughout all the surrounding regions everything they had heard and seen. The majority of the Lamanites were convinced because of the powerful evidence they had received.
All who were convinced laid down their weapons of war, along with their hatred and the traditions of their ancestors.
They gave up their lands to the Nephites.

6.text: By the end of the sixty-second year of the judges, all these things had happened. The majority of the Lamanites had become a righteous people, so much so that their righteousness was greater than that of the Nephites because of their firmness and steadiness in the faith.
Many Nephites had become hardened, unrepentant, and deeply wicked, rejecting the word of God and all the preaching and prophecy that came to them.

Nevertheless, the members of the church had great joy because of the conversion of the Lamanites and the church that had been established among them. They had fellowship with one another and rejoiced together with great joy.
Many Lamanites came to the land of Zarahemla and told the Nephites about how they had been converted, encouraging them to have faith and repent.
Many preached with extremely great power and authority, bringing many people to deep humility, making them humble followers of God and the Lamb.
Many Lamanites traveled to the land northward, and Nephi and Lehi also went to the land northward to preach to the people. And so ended the sixty-third year.
There was peace throughout the entire land. The Nephites could travel freely wherever they wanted, whether among Nephites or Lamanites.
The Lamanites could also go wherever they pleased, whether among Lamanites or Nephites. They freely traded with one another, buying, selling, and earning profits as they wished.
Both the Lamanites and Nephites became extremely wealthy. They had a great abundance of gold, silver, and all kinds of precious metals, both in the land south and in the land north.
The land south was called Lehi, and the land north was called Mulek, after the son of Zedekiah. The Lord had brought Mulek to the land north and Lehi to the land south.
Both lands had all kinds of gold, silver, and precious ore. There were also skilled craftsmen who worked with every kind of metal and refined it. This is how they became wealthy.

They grew grain in abundance, both in the north and south. They flourished greatly in both regions. They multiplied and became very strong in the land, raising many flocks and herds, including many fattened animals.
Their women worked hard, spinning thread and making all kinds of cloth, including fine linen and fabric of every type, to clothe themselves. And so the sixty-fourth year passed in peace.
In the sixty-fifth year, they also had great joy and peace, with much preaching and many prophecies about things to come. And so the sixty-fifth year passed.

In the sixty-sixth year, Cezoram was murdered by an unknown person while he sat on the judgment seat. That same year, his son, who had been chosen by the people to replace him, was also murdered. And so ended the sixty-sixth year.
At the beginning of the sixty-seventh year, the people began to grow extremely wicked again.

The Lord had blessed them with riches for so long that they had not been stirred up to anger, wars, or bloodshed. But now they began to set their hearts on their riches. They sought to get wealth so they could be lifted above one another. They began committing secret murders, robberies, and plundering to gain wealth.

These murderers and plunderers were a band that had been formed by Kishkumen and Gadianton. By this time, many even among the Nephites had joined Gadianton's band, though they were more numerous among the more wicked Lamanites. They were called Gadianton's robbers and murderers.
It was they who murdered the chief judge Cezoram and his son while they were on the judgment seat. The killers were never found.
When the Lamanites discovered there were robbers among them, they were deeply sorrowful and did everything in their power to wipe them from the face of the earth.

But Satan stirred up the hearts of most of the Nephites, so they joined with the bands of robbers and entered into their covenants and oaths, promising to protect one another no matter what, so they would never be punished for their murders, robberies, and thefts.

They had their secret signs and secret passwords so they could identify anyone who had joined their covenant. No matter what wickedness a member committed, he would not be harmed by his fellow members or anyone else in the band.
In this way, they could murder, rob, steal, and commit all kinds of wickedness, going against the laws of their country and the laws of God.

If any member of their band revealed their wickedness and evil deeds to the world, that person would be judged not by the laws of the country, but by the laws of their wicked organization, which had been established by Gadianton and Kishkumen.
These are the secret oaths and covenants that Alma had commanded his son not to make known to the world, for fear they would lead the people to destruction.

These secret oaths and covenants did not come to Gadianton from the records given to Helaman. Instead, they were placed in Gadianton's heart by the same being who tempted our first parents to eat the forbidden fruit.
That same being plotted with Cain to murder his brother Abel in secret. And he continued to plot with Cain and his followers from that time onward.

It was also that same being who put it into people's hearts to build a tower high enough to reach heaven. He led the people who came from that tower into this land, where he spread darkness and wickedness over all the land until he dragged them down to total destruction and everlasting misery.
It is that same being who put it into Gadianton's heart to continue the work of darkness and secret murder. He has been doing this from the beginning of mankind down to this time.

He is the author of all sin. He carries on his works of darkness and secret murder, passing down his plots, oaths, covenants, and plans of terrible wickedness from generation to generation, whenever he can get hold of people's hearts.

He had gained a strong hold on the hearts of the Nephites. They had become extremely wicked—most of them had turned away from righteousness, trampled God's commandments, followed their own ways, and built up idols of gold and silver for themselves.
All of these sins came upon the people in just a few years, with most of it happening in the sixty-seventh year of the judges.
Their wickedness continued to grow in the sixty-eighth year, causing great sorrow and grief among the righteous.

And so we see that the Nephites were falling into unbelief and growing in wickedness and sin, while the Lamanites were growing greatly in their knowledge of God. They began to keep His commandments and laws and to live in truth and uprightness before Him.
We see that the Spirit of the Lord began to withdraw from the Nephites because of their wickedness and the hardness of their hearts.
And we see that the Lord began to pour out His Spirit on the Lamanites because of their willingness and eagerness to believe His words.
The Lamanites hunted down Gadianton's band of robbers and preached the word of God among the more wicked of their people, until this band of robbers was completely destroyed from among the Lamanites.

On the other hand, the Nephites supported and strengthened the robbers, starting with the most wicked among them, until the robbers had spread throughout the entire Nephite land. They had even lured most of the righteous into believing in their works, sharing in their stolen goods, and joining in their secret murders and organizations.
The robbers gained complete control of the government. They trampled, beat, and turned their backs on the poor, the humble, and the meek followers of God.
And so we see that the Nephites were in a terrible state and heading toward everlasting destruction.
And so ended the sixty-eighth year of the reign of the judges over the Nephite people.

7.text: In the sixty-ninth year of the judges, Nephi, the son of Helaman, returned to the land of Zarahemla from the land northward.
He had been among the people in the land northward, preaching the word of God and prophesying many things to them.
But they rejected everything he said, so he could not stay among them and returned to his homeland.

Seeing the people in such terrible wickedness—with Gadianton robbers filling the judgment seats, having taken over the power and authority of the land, ignoring God's commandments, doing nothing right before Him, showing no justice to the people—

condemning the righteous because of their righteousness, letting the guilty and wicked go free because of their money, and holding office to rule according to their own desires so they could get wealth and worldly glory, and more easily commit adultery, steal, kill, and do whatever they wanted—
when Nephi saw all this great wickedness that had come upon the Nephites in just a few years, his heart was filled with sorrow, and he cried out in the anguish of his soul:

Oh, if only I could have lived in the days when my ancestor Nephi first came from Jerusalem! I could have rejoiced with him in the promised land. His people were easy to persuade, firm in keeping God's commandments, slow to do wrong, and quick to listen to the words of the Lord.
If only I had lived in those days, my soul would have had joy in the righteousness of my people.
But I am left to live in these times, and my soul is filled with sorrow because of the wickedness of my people.

Nephi was on a tower in his garden, which was next to the highway leading to the main marketplace in the city of Zarahemla. He had bowed down on the tower to pray, and the tower was near the garden gate by the highway.
Some people passing by saw Nephi pouring out his soul to God on the tower. They ran and told others, and crowds gathered to find out why he was mourning so deeply over the wickedness of the people.
When Nephi stood up, he saw the crowds that had gathered.
He spoke to them and said: Why have you gathered here? So I can tell you about your sins?
I climbed up on my tower to pour out my soul to God because of the tremendous sorrow in my heart over your sins!
Because of my mourning and grief, you have gathered together and you are amazed. And you really should be amazed, because you have given the devil such a strong hold on your hearts.
How could you have given in to the temptations of the one who is trying to drag your souls down to everlasting misery and endless suffering?
Repent! Repent! Why will you die? Turn, turn to the Lord your God! Why has He abandoned you?
It is because you have hardened your hearts. You will not listen to the voice of the good shepherd. You have provoked Him to anger.
Unless you repent, He will scatter you instead of gathering you, and you will become food for dogs and wild animals.
How could you have forgotten your God on the very day that He delivered you?

You have done all this to gain wealth and to be praised by others. You have set your hearts on the riches and empty things of this world, and for these things you murder, rob, steal, lie about your neighbors, and commit all kinds of wickedness.

Because of this, disaster will come to you unless you repent. If you do not repent, this great city and all the great cities around it in our land will be taken from you, and you will have no place in them. The Lord will not give you strength to stand against your enemies as He has done before.

For the Lord says: I will not show my strength to one wicked person more than another, except to those who repent of their sins and listen to my words. Therefore, my people, know that things will go better for the Lamanites than for you unless you repent.

For the Lamanites are more righteous than you. They have not sinned against the great knowledge that you have received. Therefore the Lord will be merciful to them. He will lengthen their days and increase their descendants, even while you will be completely destroyed—unless you repent.
How terrible it will be for you because of the great wickedness that has come among you! You have joined yourselves to the secret band established by Gadianton!
How terrible it will be for you because of the pride you have allowed into your hearts, which has lifted you up beyond what is good because of your extremely great wealth!
How terrible it will be for you because of your wickedness and sins!
Unless you repent, you will perish. Your lands will be taken from you, and you will be wiped from the face of the earth.

I do not say these things on my own, because it is not from myself that I know them. I know these things are true because the Lord God has made them known to me. Therefore I testify that they will happen.

8.text: After Nephi said these words, some of the judges who belonged to Gadianton's secret band became angry. They called out against him, saying to the people: Why don't you arrest this man and bring him to trial for what he has done?
Why do you let this man stand here and speak against our people and our law?
Nephi had spoken to them about how corrupt their law had become. He said many things that cannot all be written, and nothing he said went against the commandments of God.

The judges were angry with him because he spoke plainly about their secret works of darkness. But they did not dare to arrest him themselves, because they were afraid the people would cry out against them.

So they called out to the people: Why do you let this man speak against us? He is condemning all of us to destruction! He says our great cities will be taken from us and we will have no place in them.
We know that is impossible, because we are powerful and our cities are great. Our enemies can have no power over us.

By saying these things, they stirred up the people to anger against Nephi and caused arguments among them. Some people spoke up and said: Leave this man alone! He is a good man, and what he says will surely come true unless we repent.

All the judgments he has warned us about will come upon us, because we know he has told us the truth about our sins. Our sins are many, and he knows everything that will happen to us just as well as he knows about our wickedness.
If he were not a prophet, he could not have known about these things.

Those who wanted to destroy Nephi were held back by their fear, so they did not lay hands on him. Since he had gained the support of some of the people, and the rest were now afraid, he began to speak to them again.

He said: My brethren, have you not read that God gave power to one man—Moses—to strike the waters of the Red Sea, and they parted so the Israelites, our ancestors, could walk through on dry ground while the waters closed over the Egyptian armies and swallowed them up?
If God gave Moses such power, then why do you argue among yourselves and say that God has given me no power to know about the judgments that will come upon you unless you repent?
You not only deny my words, but you also deny everything spoken by our ancestors, and the words spoken by Moses, who was given such great power—including what he said about the coming of the Messiah.
Did Moses not testify that the Son of God would come? Just as he lifted up the bronze serpent in the wilderness, so would the one who was to come be lifted up.
Just as everyone who looked at that serpent would live, so everyone who looks upon the Son of God with faith and a humble heart may live—even with eternal life.
Moses was not the only one who testified of these things. All the holy prophets, from his time back to the days of Abraham, also testified of them.
Abraham saw the coming of Christ and was filled with gladness and rejoicing.

Not only did Abraham know of these things, but many people before Abraham's time were called according to the order of God—the order of His Son. This was done so that people many thousands of years before Christ's coming would know that redemption would come to them.
Since the days of Abraham, many prophets have testified of these things. The prophet Zenos testified boldly, and because of it, he was killed.

Zenock, Ezias, Isaiah, and Jeremiah also testified of these things. Jeremiah was the same prophet who testified that Jerusalem would be destroyed—and we now know that Jerusalem was destroyed according to Jeremiah's words. Then why would the Son of God not also come, as prophesied?

Will you argue that Jerusalem was not destroyed? Will you say that all of Zedekiah's sons were not killed except Mulek? Do you not see that the descendants of Zedekiah are among us, and that they were driven from Jerusalem? But this is not all.

Our ancestor Lehi was driven from Jerusalem because he testified of these things. Nephi also testified of them, and so did almost all of our ancestors down to this time. They testified of the coming of Christ and looked forward to His day with joy.
He is God, and He was with them. He showed Himself to them, and they were redeemed by Him. They gave Him glory because of what was to come.

Since you know these things and cannot deny them without lying, you have sinned by rejecting all of them, despite the many proofs you have received. You have received evidence of their truth from everything in heaven and everything on earth.

You have rejected the truth and rebelled against your holy God. Even now, instead of storing up treasures in heaven where nothing decays and nothing unclean can enter, you are storing up wrath against yourselves for the day of judgment.
Right now you are heading toward everlasting destruction because of your murders, your immoral behavior, and your wickedness. Unless you repent, it will come upon you soon.

It is already at your door. Go to the judgment seat and search, and you will find that your chief judge has been murdered. He lies in his own blood, killed by his brother, who wants to take the judgment seat.
They both belong to your secret band, whose founder is Gadianton and the evil one who seeks to destroy the souls of men.

9.text: After Nephi had spoken these words, some men among the crowd ran to the judgment seat. There were five of them, and they said to each other as they went:

Now we will find out for sure whether this man is a prophet and whether God commanded him to prophesy such amazing things to us. We don't believe he did. We don't believe he is a prophet. But if what he said about the chief judge is true—that he is dead—then we will believe the other things he has said are true.
They ran as fast as they could and arrived at the judgment seat. And there lay the chief judge, fallen to the ground in his own blood.
When they saw this, they were so astonished that they fell to the ground, because they had not believed the words Nephi had spoken about the chief judge.
But now, when they saw it with their own eyes, they believed. Fear came over them that all the judgments Nephi had spoken about might come upon the people. They trembled and fell to the ground.
Right after the judge had been murdered—stabbed by his own brother in secret, who then fled—the servants ran and told the people, raising the cry of murder among them.
The people gathered together at the judgment seat, and to their astonishment they saw those five men who had fallen to the ground.

Now the people knew nothing about the crowd that had gathered at the garden of Nephi. So they said to one another, "These men are the ones who murdered the judge, and God has struck them down so they could not escape from us."
They seized them, tied them up, and threw them into prison. A proclamation was sent out that the judge had been killed and that the murderers had been captured and imprisoned.
The next day the people gathered together to mourn and fast at the burial of the great chief judge who had been killed.
The judges who had been at the garden of Nephi and heard his words also gathered at the burial.

They asked among the people, "Where are the five men who were sent to check on the chief judge—whether he was dead?" The people answered, "We don't know about five men you say you sent, but there are five who are the murderers, and we have thrown them into prison."

The judges asked that the prisoners be brought before them. When they were brought, they turned out to be the same five men who had been sent. The judges questioned them about what had happened, and they told everything they had done, saying:
"We ran to the judgment seat, and when we saw everything just as Nephi had described, we were so astonished that we fell to the ground. When we recovered from our shock, they threw us into prison."
"As for the murder of this man, we don't know who did it. We only know that we ran and came as you asked, and he was already dead, just as Nephi had said."

Then the judges explained the situation to the people and cried out against Nephi, saying, "This Nephi must have arranged with someone to kill the judge, and then announced it to us so he could convert us to his beliefs and raise himself up as a great man, chosen by God and called a prophet."
"We will expose this man, and he will confess what he has done and reveal the true murderer of this judge."
The five men were released on the day of the burial. They rebuked the judges for the things they had said against Nephi and argued with them one by one until they left them without an answer.
Nevertheless, the judges had Nephi arrested, tied up, and brought before the crowd. They began to question him in many different ways, trying to trap him so they could condemn him to death.

They said to him, "You are part of this conspiracy. Who is the man that committed this murder? Tell us now and admit your guilt. Look, here is money for you, and we will spare your life if you tell us about the agreement you made with him."
But Nephi said to them, "You fools! You are hard-hearted, blind, and stubborn people. Do you know how long the Lord your God will allow you to continue in your sinful ways?"
"You should be crying and mourning because of the great destruction that is coming upon you right now, unless you repent."

"You say that I made an agreement with someone to murder Seezoram, our chief judge. But I tell you, the reason I told you about this was so you could see for yourselves. It was a witness to you that I know about the wickedness and terrible things happening among you."
"And because I have done this, you say I made a deal with a man to do this. Because I showed you this sign, you are angry with me and want to take my life."
"Now I will show you another sign, and see if you will try to destroy me for this one too."
"I say to you: Go to the house of Seantum, who is the brother of Seezoram, and say to him—"
"'Has Nephi, the so-called prophet who prophesies so much evil about this people, made an agreement with you to murder Seezoram, your brother?'"
"And he will say to you, 'No.'"
"Then you will say to him, 'Have you murdered your brother?'"
"He will stand there in fear, not knowing what to say. He will deny it and act as if he is surprised. But he will tell you he is innocent."
"But you will examine him, and you will find blood on the edges of his cloak."
"When you see this, you will say, 'Where did this blood come from? Don't we know that it is the blood of your brother?'"
"Then he will tremble and turn pale, as if death itself had come upon him."
"Then you will say, 'Because of this fear and paleness on your face, we know that you are guilty.'"
"Then even greater fear will come upon him, and he will confess to you and no longer deny that he committed this murder."
"Then he will tell you that I, Nephi, know nothing about this matter except what was given to me by the power of God. And then you will know that I am an honest man, sent to you by God."
They went and did exactly as Nephi had told them. And the words he had spoken were true: Seantum first denied it, just as Nephi said he would, and then confessed, just as Nephi said he would.
Seantum was proven to be the actual murderer, so the five men were set free, and Nephi was also released.
Some of the Nephites believed Nephi's words. Others believed because of the testimony of the five men, who had been converted while they were in prison.
There were some among the people who said that Nephi was a prophet.

And there were others who said, "He must be a god, because only a god could know all things. He has told us the thoughts of our hearts and revealed things to us. He even identified the true murderer of our chief judge."

10.text: A division arose among the people, and they split apart and went their separate ways, leaving Nephi standing alone in the middle of them.
Nephi went toward his own house, thinking deeply about the things the Lord had shown him.
As he was thinking—feeling very discouraged because of the wickedness of the Nephite people, their secret plots, their murders, their robberies, and all kinds of sin—a voice came to him, saying:

"Blessed are you, Nephi, because of the things you have done. I have watched how you have tirelessly declared the word I gave you to this people. You have not been afraid of them, and you have not tried to save your own life, but have sought my will and kept my commandments."

"And because you have done this with such tireless dedication, I will bless you forever. I will make you mighty in word and action, in faith and in works. Everything will be done for you according to your word, because you will not ask for anything that goes against my will."

"You are Nephi, and I am God. I declare to you in the presence of my angels that you will have power over this people. You will be able to strike the earth with famine, disease, and destruction, according to the wickedness of this people."
"I give you power so that whatever you seal on earth will be sealed in heaven, and whatever you release on earth will be released in heaven. And so you will have power among this people."
"If you say to this temple, 'Be torn in two,' it will be done."
"If you say to this mountain, 'Be thrown down and become flat,' it will be done."
"And if you say that God should strike this people, it will happen."
"Now I command you to go and declare to this people that the Lord God, the Almighty, says: Unless you repent, you will be struck down, even to the point of destruction."

After the Lord had spoken these words to Nephi, he stopped and did not go to his own house. Instead, he returned to the crowds that were scattered across the land and began to declare the word of the Lord to them, warning them about the destruction that would come if they did not repent.
Even though Nephi had performed that great miracle by telling them about the death of the chief judge, the people hardened their hearts and would not listen to the words of the Lord.
So Nephi declared the word of the Lord to them, saying, "Unless you repent, the Lord says you will be struck down even to the point of destruction."
After Nephi had declared the word to them, they still hardened their hearts and would not listen. They spoke against him and tried to seize him to throw him into prison.
But the power of God was with him, and they could not take him to put him in prison, because the Spirit carried him away from among them.
He went from crowd to crowd in the Spirit, declaring the word of God until he had declared it to all the people or sent it out among everyone.
But they would not listen to his words. Conflicts began among them, and they became so divided that they started killing each other with swords.
And so ended the seventy-first year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.

11.text: In the seventy-second year of the reign of the judges, the conflicts increased until there were wars throughout the entire land among all the people of Nephi.
It was the secret band of robbers who carried on this work of destruction and wickedness. This war lasted all that year, and it continued into the seventy-third year as well.
In this year, Nephi cried out to the Lord, saying:

"O Lord, do not let this people be destroyed by the sword. Instead, O Lord, let there be a famine in the land to remind them of the Lord their God. Perhaps then they will repent and turn back to you."

And it happened just as Nephi had asked. A great famine came upon the land among all the people of Nephi. In the seventy-fourth year, the famine continued, and the killing by the sword stopped, but the suffering from hunger became severe.

This destruction also continued in the seventy-fifth year. The earth was so dry that it would not produce grain in the growing season. The whole earth was affected, among the Lamanites as well as the Nephites. They were struck so severely that thousands died in the more wicked parts of the land.
When the people saw that they were about to die from famine, they began to remember the Lord their God. They began to remember the words of Nephi.

The people began to plead with their chief judges and leaders to say to Nephi, "We know that you are a man of God. Please pray to the Lord our God to take away this famine, so that everything you have spoken about our destruction does not come true."
The judges spoke to Nephi as the people had asked. When Nephi saw that the people had repented and humbled themselves by wearing sackcloth, he prayed to the Lord again, saying:
"O Lord, this people is repenting. They have wiped out the band of Gadianton from among them so that it no longer exists. They have buried their secret plans in the earth."
"Now, O Lord, because of their humility, will you turn away your anger? Let your anger be satisfied by the destruction of those wicked men you have already destroyed."
"O Lord, will you turn away your anger, your fierce anger, and cause this famine to end in this land?"
"O Lord, will you hear me and cause it to be done according to my words? Send rain upon the earth so it can produce its fruit and grain in the growing season."

"O Lord, you listened to my words when I said, 'Let there be a famine so the killing by the sword might stop.' And I know you will listen to my words now, because you said, 'If this people repents, I will spare them.'"
"And Lord, you can see that they have repented because of the famine, the suffering, and the destruction that has come upon them."
"And now, O Lord, will you turn away your anger and test them again to see if they will serve you? If they will, O Lord, you can bless them according to the promises you have made."
In the seventy-sixth year, the Lord turned away his anger from the people and caused rain to fall on the earth. The land produced fruit in its season and grain in its season.

The people rejoiced and glorified God. The whole land was filled with joy. They no longer tried to destroy Nephi but honored him as a great prophet and a man of God who had great power and authority given to him from God.
And Lehi, his brother, was just as righteous as he was in all things.

The people of Nephi began to prosper again in the land. They rebuilt their ruined places and began to multiply and spread until they covered the whole face of the land, both north and south, from the west sea to the east sea.

The seventy-sixth year ended in peace. The seventy-seventh year began in peace. The church spread throughout the whole land. Most of the people, both Nephites and Lamanites, belonged to the church, and they had great peace in the land. And so ended the seventy-seventh year.
They also had peace in the seventy-eighth year, except for a few disagreements about points of doctrine that had been taught by the prophets.

In the seventy-ninth year, much conflict began. But Nephi, Lehi, and many of their fellow believers who understood the true points of doctrine—receiving many revelations daily—preached to the people and put an end to the conflict that same year.

In the eightieth year of the reign of the judges, a number of Nephite dissenters who had gone over to the Lamanites years earlier and taken the name of Lamanites, along with some actual descendants of the Lamanites who were stirred up to anger by these dissenters, started a war against their fellow people.
They committed murder and robbery, then retreated into the mountains and wilderness and secret hideouts where they could not be found. Their numbers grew daily as more dissenters joined them.
Over time—not many years—they became a very large band of robbers. They found all the secret plans of Gadianton, and so they became known as the robbers of Gadianton.
These robbers caused great destruction among the people of Nephi and also among the people of the Lamanites.
It became necessary to stop this work of destruction, so they sent an army of strong men into the wilderness and mountains to find this band of robbers and destroy them.
But that same year, the army was driven back to their own lands. And so ended the eightieth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.
At the beginning of the eighty-first year, they went out again against the band of robbers and destroyed many of them. But they also suffered heavy losses.
They were forced to return from the wilderness and mountains to their own lands because there were so many robbers filling the mountains and wilderness.

And so that year ended. The robbers continued to grow in strength until they could stand against the entire armies of both the Nephites and the Lamanites. They caused great fear among all the people across the land.
They attacked many parts of the land and caused great destruction. They killed many and carried others away captive into the wilderness, especially the women and children.
This great evil, which came upon the people because of their wickedness, stirred them up again to remember the Lord their God.
And so ended the eighty-first year of the reign of the judges.

In the eighty-second year, they began again to forget the Lord their God. In the eighty-third year, they began to grow strong in wickedness. And in the eighty-fourth year, they did not change their ways.
In the eighty-fifth year, they became more and more proud and wicked, and so they were becoming ripe for destruction once again.
And so ended the eighty-fifth year.

12.text: And so we can see how unreliable and unsteady the hearts of people are. Yet we can also see that the Lord, in his great and infinite goodness, blesses and helps those who put their trust in him.

At the very time when God blesses his people—increasing their fields, flocks, and herds, and giving them gold, silver, and every kind of precious thing; sparing their lives and delivering them from their enemies; softening the hearts of their enemies so they will not wage war against them; doing everything for their well-being and happiness—that is exactly when they harden their hearts, forget the Lord their God, and trample the Holy One under their feet. They do this because of their comfort and their great prosperity.
And so we see that unless the Lord disciplines his people with many hardships—with death, fear, famine, and all kinds of suffering—they will not remember him.

How foolish, how empty, how evil, and how quick to do wrong and how slow to do good are the children of men! How quick they are to listen to the evil one and to set their hearts on the worthless things of the world!

How quick they are to be puffed up with pride! How quick to brag and to do all kinds of wicked things! And how slow they are to remember the Lord their God and to listen to his guidance—how slow to walk in the paths of wisdom!
They do not want the Lord their God, who created them, to rule and reign over them. Despite his great goodness and mercy toward them, they disregard his guidance and refuse to let him lead them.
How insignificant are the children of men! They are even less than the dust of the earth.
For the dust of the earth moves back and forth and splits apart at the command of our great and everlasting God.
At his voice, the hills and mountains tremble and shake.
By the power of his voice, they are broken apart and become smooth, like a valley.
By the power of his voice, the whole earth shakes.
By the power of his voice, the very foundations of the earth rock, even to their center.
If he says to the earth, "Move," it moves.
If he says to the earth, "Turn back so that the day is lengthened for many hours," it is done.
And so, according to his word, the earth turns back, and it appears to people that the sun stands still. And this is true, because it is the earth that moves and not the sun.
And if he says to the waters of the great deep, "Dry up," it is done.
If he says to a mountain, "Rise up and fall upon that city so that it is buried," it is done.
And if a person hides a treasure in the earth and the Lord says, "Let it be cursed because of the wickedness of the one who hid it," it will be cursed.
And if the Lord says, "Be cursed, so that no one will find you from now on and forever," no one will ever find it.
And if the Lord says to someone, "Because of your sins, you will be cursed forever," it will be done.
And if the Lord says, "Because of your sins, you will be cut off from my presence," he will cause it to happen.

And how terrible it will be for those to whom he says this, for it will happen to those who choose to do evil, and they cannot be saved. For this reason—so that people can be saved—repentance has been declared.
Therefore, blessed are those who repent and listen to the voice of the Lord their God, for they are the ones who will be saved.
And may God grant, in his great fullness, that people might be led to repentance and good works, so that they might be restored to grace for grace, according to their works.
And I wish that all people could be saved. But we read that in the great and last day, some will be cast out—cast off from the presence of the Lord.

They will be given over to a state of endless misery, fulfilling the words that say: Those who have done good will have everlasting life, and those who have done evil will have everlasting punishment. And so it is. Amen.

13.text: In the eighty-sixth year, the Nephites still remained in wickedness—great wickedness—while the Lamanites carefully kept the commandments of God according to the law of Moses.

In this year, a Lamanite named Samuel came to the land of Zarahemla and began to preach to the people. He preached repentance for many days, but they cast him out, and he was about to return to his own land.
But the voice of the Lord came to him, telling him to go back and prophesy to the people whatever the Lord put into his heart.
They would not let him enter the city, so he climbed up on the city wall, stretched out his hand, and cried out with a loud voice, prophesying to the people whatever the Lord put into his heart.

He said to them, "I, Samuel, a Lamanite, speak the words of the Lord that he puts into my heart. He has told me to say to this people that the sword of justice hangs over you. Within four hundred years, the sword of justice will fall upon this people."

"Terrible destruction awaits this people, and it is surely coming. Nothing can save this people except repentance and faith in the Lord Jesus Christ, who will surely come into the world and suffer many things and be killed for his people."
"An angel of the Lord declared this to me, and he brought joyful news to my soul. I was sent to declare it to you also, so you could have this joyful news, but you would not receive me."

"Therefore, the Lord says: Because of the hardness of the hearts of the Nephite people, unless they repent, I will take away my word from them, withdraw my Spirit from them, and allow them to suffer. I will turn the hearts of their brethren against them."
"Four hundred years will not pass before I cause them to be struck down. I will visit them with the sword, famine, and disease."

"I will visit them in my fierce anger, and there will be people of the fourth generation of your enemies who will live to see your complete destruction. This will surely happen unless you repent, says the Lord. Those of the fourth generation will witness your destruction."
"But if you repent and return to the Lord your God, I will turn away my anger, says the Lord. Blessed are those who repent and turn to me, but how terrible it will be for those who do not repent."

"How terrible it will be for this great city of Zarahemla! It is only because of the righteous people here that it is spared. I can see, says the Lord, that many—even most of the people in this great city—will harden their hearts against me."
"But blessed are those who repent, for I will spare them. If it were not for the righteous in this great city, I would send fire down from heaven to destroy it."

"It is for the sake of the righteous that it is spared. But the time is coming, says the Lord, when you will cast out the righteous from among you. Then you will be ripe for destruction. How terrible it will be for this great city because of the wickedness and the terrible things in it."
"And how terrible it will be for the city of Gideon because of the wickedness and terrible things in it."
"And how terrible it will be for all the cities throughout the land that are held by the Nephites, because of the wickedness and terrible things in them."
"A curse will come upon the land, says the Lord of Hosts, because of the people who live on it—because of their wickedness and their terrible deeds."

"The Lord of Hosts, our great and true God, says that whoever hides treasures in the earth will never find them again because of the great curse on the land, unless he is a righteous person who hides it up for the Lord."

"For the Lord says, 'I want them to dedicate their treasures to me. Cursed are those who do not dedicate their treasures to me, for no one stores up treasures for me except the righteous. Whoever does not dedicate his treasures to me—cursed is he, and the treasure too, and no one will recover it because of the curse on the land.'"

"The day will come when they will hide their treasures because they have set their hearts on riches. Because they have set their hearts on wealth, they will hide their treasures when they flee from their enemies. Because they will not dedicate them to me, cursed be they and their treasures too. In that day they will be struck down, says the Lord."

"Listen, you people of this great city, and hear my words! Listen to the words the Lord says. He says you are cursed because of your riches, and your riches are also cursed because you have set your hearts on them and have not listened to the words of him who gave them to you."

"You do not remember the Lord your God for the things he has blessed you with, but you always remember your riches—not to thank the Lord your God for them. Your hearts are not turned toward the Lord. Instead, they swell with great pride, leading to bragging, envy, conflict, hatred, persecution, murder, and all kinds of wickedness."
"For this reason, the Lord God has caused a curse to come upon the land and upon your riches, because of your sins."

"How terrible it will be for this people because of the time that has arrived when you cast out the prophets, mock them, throw stones at them, kill them, and do all kinds of evil to them, just as people did in ancient times."
"And when you talk, you say, 'If we had lived in the days of our ancestors, we would not have killed the prophets. We would not have stoned them and cast them out.'"

"You are worse than they were! As the Lord lives, if a prophet comes among you and declares the word of the Lord, testifying of your sins and wrongdoings, you become angry with him. You cast him out and look for every way to destroy him. You say he is a false prophet, a sinner, and of the devil, because he testifies that your deeds are evil."

"But if someone comes among you and says, 'Do this, and there is nothing wrong with it; do that, and you won't suffer for it'—if he says, 'Follow the pride of your own hearts; follow the desires of your own eyes, and do whatever your heart wants'—you will welcome him and call him a prophet."

"You will praise him and give him your possessions. You will give him your gold and silver and dress him in expensive clothing. Because he speaks flattering words to you and says everything is fine, you will find no fault with him."

"O you wicked and twisted generation! You hardened and stubborn people, how long do you think the Lord will put up with you? How long will you let yourselves be led by foolish and blind guides? How long will you choose darkness rather than light?"
"The anger of the Lord is already burning against you. He has cursed the land because of your wickedness."
"The time is coming when he will curse your riches so that they become slippery and you cannot hold on to them. In the days of your poverty, you will not be able to keep them."

"In the days of your poverty, you will cry out to the Lord, but you will cry in vain, because your ruin will have already come upon you and your destruction will be certain. Then you will weep and howl in that day, says the Lord of Hosts. Then you will cry out and say:"

"'If only I had repented and had not killed the prophets and stoned them and cast them out!' In that day you will say, 'If only we had remembered the Lord our God when he gave us our riches! Then they would not have become slippery so that we lost them. Our riches are gone from us.'"
"'We set a tool down here and the next day it is gone. Our swords are taken from us on the day we look for them for battle.'"
"'We hid our treasures and they have slipped away from us because of the curse on the land.'"
"'If only we had repented when the word of the Lord came to us! The land is cursed, and everything has become slippery, and we cannot hold on to anything.'"

"'We are surrounded by demons! We are encircled by the angels of the one who has tried to destroy our souls. Our sins are great. O Lord, can you not turn away your anger from us?' This will be your language in those days."

"But your days of testing are past. You have put off the day of your salvation until it is forever too late, and your destruction is certain. You have spent all the days of your lives seeking what you could not obtain, and you have looked for happiness in doing evil, which goes against the very nature of the righteousness found in our great and Eternal God."
"O you people of the land, I wish you would hear my words! I pray that the anger of the Lord will be turned away from you and that you will repent and be saved."

14.text: Samuel the Lamanite prophesied many more things that cannot all be written down.
He said to them, "I give you a sign: In five more years, the Son of God will come to redeem all those who believe in his name."
"And I will give you this sign at the time of his coming: There will be great lights in the sky, so that on the night before he comes there will be no darkness—it will look like daytime."

"There will be one day, a night, and another day, as if it were all one day with no night. This will be a sign to you. You will know by the rising and setting of the sun that two days and a night have passed, yet the night will not be dark. It will be the night before he is born."
"And a new star will appear, one like you have never seen before. This will also be a sign to you."
"And that is not all—there will be many signs and wonders in the sky."
"You will all be so amazed and filled with wonder that you will fall to the ground."
"Whoever believes in the Son of God will have everlasting life."

"The Lord has commanded me through his angel to come and tell you these things. He has commanded me to prophesy these things to you. He said to me, 'Cry out to this people: Repent and prepare the way of the Lord.'"

"And now, because I am a Lamanite and have spoken the words the Lord commanded me—and because what I said was hard for you to hear—you are angry with me. You want to destroy me, and you have cast me out from among you."

"But you will hear my words, because this is why I have come up on the walls of this city: so you can hear and learn about the judgments of God that await you because of your sins, and so you can know about the conditions of repentance."

"And so you can learn about the coming of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, the Father of heaven and earth, the Creator of all things from the beginning—and so you can know the signs of his coming, so that you will believe in his name."
"And if you believe in his name, you will repent of all your sins, so that you can receive forgiveness of them through his merits."
"And now, I give you another sign—a sign of his death."

"For he must surely die so that salvation can come. It is necessary and right that he die, to bring about the resurrection of the dead, so that through this, people can be brought back into the presence of the Lord."

"This death brings about the resurrection and redeems all of humanity from the first death—the spiritual death. Because of the fall of Adam, all of humanity was cut off from the presence of the Lord and is considered dead, both in physical things and in spiritual things."
"But the resurrection of Christ redeems all of humanity and brings them back into the presence of the Lord."

"And it brings about the opportunity for repentance. Whoever repents is not cut down and thrown into the fire. But whoever does not repent is cut down and thrown into the fire, and a spiritual death comes upon them again—a second death—for they are cut off once more from things of righteousness."
"Therefore repent, repent! Otherwise, by knowing these things and not doing them, you will bring condemnation upon yourselves and be brought down to this second death."

"But as I told you about another sign—a sign of his death—on the day he suffers death, the sun will be darkened and will refuse to give its light to you. The moon and the stars will also be darkened. There will be no light on the face of this land from the time he dies for a period of three days, until the time he rises again from the dead."

"At the moment he dies, there will be thunder and lightning for many hours, and the earth will shake and tremble. The rocks on the surface of this earth—those above the ground and those below it, which you know are solid, mostly one solid mass—will be broken apart."
"They will be torn in two, and from then on will be found in seams, cracks, and broken pieces across the face of the whole earth, both above and beneath."
"There will be great storms, and many mountains will be leveled flat like a valley, and many places that are now valleys will become tall mountains."
"Many roads will be broken up, and many cities will become empty and deserted."
"Many graves will be opened, and many of their dead will come forth. Many saints will appear to many people."
"The angel has told me these things. He said there would be thunder and lightning for many hours."
"He told me that while the thunder, lightning, and storms lasted, these things would happen, and that darkness would cover the whole earth for three days."

"The angel said to me that many will see even greater things than these, so that they might believe that these signs and wonders would come to pass across the whole face of this land, so that there would be no reason for unbelief among the people."
"This is so that whoever will believe might be saved, and whoever will not believe will receive a righteous judgment. And if they are condemned, they bring their own condemnation upon themselves."

"And now remember, remember, my brethren, that whoever perishes, perishes by his own choice. And whoever does evil, does it to himself. For you are free. You are allowed to act for yourselves. God has given you knowledge, and he has made you free."

"He has given you the ability to know good from evil, and he has given you the ability to choose life or death. You can do good and have good things restored to you, or you can do evil and have evil restored to you."

15.text: "And now, my beloved brethren, I declare to you that unless you repent, your homes will be left to you empty and desolate."

"Unless you repent, your women will have great cause to mourn when they are nursing their babies, because you will try to flee and there will be no place of refuge. How terrible it will be for those who are pregnant, for they will be weighed down and unable to flee. They will be trampled and left to die."

"How terrible it will be for this people called the people of Nephi, unless they repent when they see all the signs and wonders that will be shown to them!
