For they have been a chosen people of the Lord. He has loved the people of Nephi, and he has disciplined them. In the days of their wickedness, he disciplined them because he loves them."

"But my brethren, the Lord has been displeased with the Lamanites because their actions have always been evil, and this is because of the sinful traditions of their fathers. But salvation has come to them through the preaching of the Nephites, and for this purpose the Lord has prolonged their days."
"And I want you to notice that most of them are doing their duty. They walk carefully before God and observe his commandments, statutes, and judgments according to the law of Moses."
"Most of them are doing this, and they are working with tireless effort to bring the rest of their people to the knowledge of the truth. Many are added to their numbers every day."

"And you know from your own experience that as many of them as come to the knowledge of the truth and learn about the wicked and terrible traditions of their fathers, and are led to believe the holy scriptures—the prophecies of the holy prophets, which are written—which lead them to faith in the Lord and to repentance, which faith and repentance bring a change of heart—"
"As many as have come to this, you know are firm and steadfast in the faith and in the things that have set them free."

"You also know that they have buried their weapons of war and are afraid to take them up again in case they might sin. You can see that they are so afraid of sinning that they will allow themselves to be struck down and killed by their enemies rather than lift their swords against them. This is because of their faith in Christ."
"And because of their steadfastness in what they believe—because of their firmness once they have been enlightened—the Lord will bless them and prolong their days, despite their past wrongdoing."

"Even if they fall into unbelief, the Lord will prolong their days until the time spoken of by our fathers and by the prophet Zenos and many other prophets concerning the restoration of our brethren the Lamanites to the knowledge of the truth."

"I tell you that in the latter times, the Lord's promises have been extended to our brethren the Lamanites. Despite the many hardships they will face—despite being driven back and forth across the earth, hunted, struck, and scattered with no place of refuge—the Lord will be merciful to them."
"This is according to prophecy: they will be brought back to the true knowledge, which is the knowledge of their Redeemer, their great and true shepherd, and they will be numbered among his sheep."
"Therefore I say to you, it will be better for them than for you, unless you repent."

"For if the mighty works that have been shown to you had been shown to them—those who fell into unbelief because of the traditions of their fathers—you can see for yourselves that they never would have fallen into unbelief again."
"Therefore the Lord says, 'I will not completely destroy them, but I will cause that in the day of my wisdom they will return to me again, says the Lord.'"

"And now the Lord says concerning the people of the Nephites: 'If they will not repent and follow my will, I will completely destroy them, says the Lord, because of their unbelief, despite the many mighty works I have done among them. As surely as the Lord lives, these things will happen, says the Lord.'"

16.text: Many people heard the words of Samuel the Lamanite that he spoke from the walls of the city. Those who believed his words went and looked for Nephi. When they found him, they confessed their sins without holding back, wanting to be baptized unto the Lord.

But those who did not believe Samuel's words were angry with him. They threw stones at him on the wall, and many shot arrows at him as he stood on the wall. But the Spirit of the Lord was with him, and they could not hit him with their stones or their arrows.
When they saw that they could not hit him, many more believed his words and went to Nephi to be baptized.
For Nephi was baptizing, prophesying, and preaching, calling the people to repentance, showing signs and wonders, and working miracles among the people so they would know that Christ must come soon.

He told them about things that would happen soon, so they would know and remember when those things came to pass that they had been told about them beforehand. This was so they would believe. Therefore, those who believed Samuel's words went to Nephi to be baptized, for they came repenting and confessing their sins.

But most of the people did not believe Samuel's words. When they saw they could not hit him with their stones and arrows, they called out to their captains, saying, "Seize this man and tie him up! He has a devil, and it is because of the power of the devil in him that we cannot hit him with our stones and arrows. So take him and tie him up, and get rid of him!"
As they went to grab him, he jumped down from the wall, fled out of their lands, and went back to his own country, where he began to preach and prophesy among his own people.
He was never heard from again among the Nephites, and that is how things stood with the people.
And so ended the eighty-sixth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.
And so ended the eighty-seventh year of the reign of the judges as well, with most of the people remaining in their pride and wickedness, and the smaller portion walking more carefully before God.
These were also the conditions in the eighty-eighth year of the reign of the judges.

There was little change in the affairs of the people, except that they began to be even more hardened in wickedness and to do more and more things that were against the commandments of God, in the eighty-ninth year of the reign of the judges.
But in the ninetieth year of the reign of the judges, great signs were given to the people, and wonders occurred. The words of the prophets began to be fulfilled.
Angels appeared to men—wise men—and declared glad tidings of great joy to them. In this year, the scriptures began to be fulfilled.
Nevertheless, the people began to harden their hearts—all except the most believing among them, both Nephites and Lamanites. They began to rely on their own strength and their own wisdom, saying:
"They may have guessed some things right among so many predictions, but we know that all these great and marvelous works they have spoken about cannot possibly happen."
And they began to argue among themselves, saying:

"It doesn't make sense that a being called Christ would come. If he is the Son of God, the Father of heaven and earth, as has been said, why won't he show himself to us as well as to those in Jerusalem?"
"Why won't he show himself in this land just as he will in the land of Jerusalem?"

"We know that this is a wicked tradition handed down to us by our fathers, to make us believe in some great and marvelous thing that will happen—but not among us; instead, in a land far away, a land we don't know about. That way they can keep us ignorant, because we can't see with our own eyes whether these things are true."

"And they will use the cunning and mysterious tricks of the evil one to create some great mystery we can't understand, which will keep us under their control, servants to their words and servants to them. We depend on them to teach us the word, and that is how they will keep us ignorant all our lives, if we submit to them."

The people imagined many more things in their hearts that were foolish and empty. They were very disturbed, because Satan stirred them up to do evil continually. He went about spreading rumors and causing conflicts throughout the whole land so he could harden the hearts of the people against what was good and against what was to come.
Despite the signs and wonders that were performed among the people of the Lord and the many miracles they did, Satan gained a strong hold on the hearts of the people throughout the whole land.
And so ended the ninetieth year of the reign of the judges over the people of Nephi.
And so ended the book of Helaman, according to the record of Helaman and his sons.
